name: Golang
scope: A go developer with BASIC proficiency in Golang, typically represents 1вЂ“3 years of experience, with the ability to build, run, and test small applications using core tools like `go build`, `go run`, and `go test`. They grasp language fundamentals (variables, loops, conditionals) and data structures (arrays, slices, maps), have a basic understanding of pointers, and use standard libraries (`fmt`, `strings`, `errors`, `net/http`) proficiently. Their concurrency skills involve goroutines and channels for simple parallel tasks, occasionally using `sync.WaitGroup`, though advanced patterns are beyond their comfort. They rely on `go.mod` and `go.sum` for dependency management, can integrate basic third-party libraries (e.g., Gin, Echo for HTTP routing, Logrus for logging), and format code with `gofmt` or `goimports`. They write unit tests using the `testing` package but may not be familiar with advanced mocking or coverage tools. They apply simple design principles, follow basic code organization practices, and can perform minimal debugging with logs or `fmt.Println`. Their error handling aligns with Go best practices (error returns, nil checks), and they might implement simple retry or fallback logic. They can handle basic CRUD operations using `database/sql` or a simple ORM, but lack deep knowledge of database performance tuning. Refactoring at this level is usually straightforward, and they may need guidance on complex architecture, high-performance optimization, or less-common frameworks. While they manage day-to-day tasks effectively, they typically look to more experienced colleagues for deeper architectural and concurrency decisions.

name: Kafka
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Kafka as a competency should be able to describe the full log-based architectureвЂ”topics, partitions, offsets, leaderвЂ“follower replication, ISR, high-water-mark, ordering guarantees, retention and compaction policiesвЂ”plus delivery semantics (at-most/least/exactly-once) and the roles of brokers, ZooKeeper or KRaft controllers, producers and consumers. They can code producers/consumers in official client SDKs (Java, Scala, Kotlin, Python, Go, .NET) using configs such as bootstrap.servers, serializers/Serdes, acks, retries, linger.ms, batch.size, compression.type, idempotence, transactional.id, group.id, enable.auto.commit, auto.offset.reset, session.timeout.ms and max.poll.* while handling synchronous/asynchronous sends, callbacks, graceful shutdown and simple Avro/Protobuf/JSON-Schema Registry integration. They create, alter and delete topics with kafka-topics.sh, kafka-configs.sh, kafka-consumer-groups.sh, kcat/kafkacat, AdminClient or REST, choosing replication-factor, partition-count, cleanup.policy, retention.ms/bytes, min.insync.replicas and understanding unclean.leader.election impacts. They spin up and manage single or small multi-broker clusters via local installs, Docker Compose, containers or managed services like Confluent Cloud, AWS MSK and Azure Event Hubs, interpret broker logs, observe leader distribution, ISR, consumer-group lag and reset offsets when required. They tune basicsвЂ”segment.bytes, segment.ms, retention, compression, producer batching, num.network/io/handler threads, broker heap (4вЂ“8 GB) and page cache awarenessвЂ”to reach predictable throughput and latency for small-scale loads. They enable PLAINTEXT for dev and configure TLS certificates, SASL/PLAIN or SASL/SCRAM users, truststore/keystore files and ACLs for simple prod isolation. They export JMX metrics from brokers and clients to Prometheus (jmx_exporter) or similar, build dashboards for throughput, latency, lag, under-replicated/offline partitions and request queues, and can triage leader-not-available errors, rebalance storms, disk-full or lag explosions. They use partitioning strategies (key-hash, round-robin, custom) to reason about parallelism and ordering, and understand consumer-group rebalancing phases and cooperative vs eager protocols. They can deploy and configure out-of-the-box Kafka Connect source/sink connectors (file, JDBC, S3, Elastic, etc.) with converters, and know where Kafka Streams, ksqlDB, Flink or Spark Structured Streaming fit without yet mastering them. They embed topic scripts, client configs and monitoring hooks into CI/CD and IaC (Docker Compose, Terraform, Helm), write/run unit tests, review peer code for correctness, and participate in incident calls with first-line Kafka diagnostics. While competent at day-to-day dev/ops for clusters up to a few TB, they are not yet accountable for multi-AZ sizing, cross-DC replication, deep JVM/OS tuning, advanced transactions or complex stream-processing topologies.
long_scope:
Kafka вЂ“ BASIC Proficiency (в‰€ 1вЂ“2 years of hands-on use)

Purpose of this scope  
A BASIC-level practitioner is expected to be self-sufficient for day-to-day development and light-weight operations on small-to-medium Kafka deployments, to extend and maintain code that already uses Kafka, and to assist senior team members during more complex cluster, security or performance activities.  Depth in fundamentals and correctness of mental models are paramount; sophisticated, large-scale, or niche capabilities are deferred to higher proficiency levels.

The scope below definesвЂ”without relying on illustrative вЂњexamplesвЂќвЂ”what the practitioner must demonstrably know, build, configure, operate, and troubleshoot when working with Apache Kafka and its immediately adjacent tooling.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
1. Architectural & Conceptual Foundations
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў Complete mental model of the Kafka log abstraction: topics, partitions, offsets, leaderвЂ“follower replication, in-sync replicas, high-water-mark, and ordering guarantees confined to a single partition.  
вЂў End-to-end data-flow path: producer в†’ broker network layer в†’ log segment files в†’ consumer fetch в†’ offset management.  
вЂў Distinction, roles and lifecycle of brokers, ZooKeeper or Kraft controllers (awareness of transition), client libraries, and cluster metadata dissemination; ability to articulate how producers/consumers discover leaders.  
вЂў Recognition of delivery semantics (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once) and the configurable points at which message loss or duplication is prevented or permitted.  
вЂў Partitioning strategy mechanics (hashing on key, round-robin, custom) and its impact on parallelism and ordering.  
вЂў Disk-backed commit-log behaviour: segment creation, time/size based retention, compaction eligibility, tombstone concept.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
2. Producer & Consumer Development Capability
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў Writing, reading and unit-testing code that uses a supported client SDK (Java / Kotlin / Scala / Python / Go / .NET) and idiomatic APIs to publish and subscribe.  
вЂў Setting and validating baseline client configurations: bootstrap servers, key/value serializers or Serdes, acks, retries, linger.ms, batch.size, compression.type, max.in.flight.requests, transactional.id (awareness), group.id, enable.auto.commit, auto.offset.reset, session.timeout.ms, max.poll.records.  
вЂў Implementing synchronous and asynchronous send paths, callback handling, basic error handling, and graceful shutdown that commits final offsets.  
вЂў Incorporation of simple schema handling (plain String/JSON) and awareness of Schema Registry workflow; can plug Avro/Protobuf/JSON-Schema serializers when provided a schema artefact.  
вЂў Use of idempotent producer flag and safe-producer parameter set when required to avoid duplicates.  
вЂў Instrumentation of client metrics (JMX or language-native) for throughput, latency, error, and lag awareness.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
3. Topic & Cluster Administration (Day-to-Day)
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў Creating, describing, altering and deleting topics as needed for feature delivery, including explicit selection of replication factor, partition count, cleanup.policy, retention.ms/bytes.  
вЂў Performing rolling addition of partitions to an existing topic while recognising consequences for key distribution and ordering.  
вЂў Command-line fluency with bundled admin tools (kafka-topics.sh, kafka-configs.sh, kafka-consumer-groups.sh, kafka-reassign-partitions.sh where applicable), and equivalent REST or AdminClient usage.  
вЂў Spinning up, starting, stopping and validating a single-node or small multi-broker cluster on local workstation, Docker Compose, or cloud test environment.  
вЂў Reading broker logs, interpreting common WARN/ERROR messages, and correlating them with operational symptoms.  
вЂў Observing replica status (ISR), cluster ID, controller broker, partition leadership distribution and consumer-group lag.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
4. Reliability, Fault-Tolerance & Data Guarantees
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў Selection of replication factor, min.insync.replicas, unclean.leader.election.enabled to meet non-catastrophic data-loss criteria.  
вЂў Understanding leader election events, controller failover, and impact on producers/consumers.  
вЂў Manual or automated offset reset (earliest, latest, offset value) during reprocessing or backfills.  
вЂў Comprehension of consumer-group rebalancing phases, causes, and back-pressure effects (max.poll.interval.ms, cooperative vs eager rebalancesвЂ”awareness).  
вЂў Ability to calculate and reason through end-to-end latency, throughput, and lag figures under typical small-scale workloads.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
5. Configuration, Performance & Capacity Tuning (Foundational)
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў Choosing segment.bytes, segment.ms, retention.ms/bytes, log.cleaner threads, and compression type to balance disk, CPU, and network utilisation for clusters up to a few terabytes.  
вЂў Applying baseline optimisation of producer side batching and compression for expected message sizes (<1 MB).  
вЂў Basic comprehension of broker heap, page cache and disk I/O interplay; sizing guidelines (heap в‰€ 4вЂ“8 GB, OS page cache rule of thumb).  
вЂў Familiarity with controller.global-thread-pool, request.handler.threads, num.network.threads, num.io.threads, and when they may need adjustment in modest clusters.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
6. Security Fundamentals (Awareness & Simple Enablement)
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў Catalog of Kafka security layers: TLS encryption, SASL/PLAIN, SASL/SCRAM, SASL/OAUTHBEARER, ACLs; can enable PLAINTEXT for dev and reason about production hardening workflow.  
вЂў Enabling TLS on broker listeners and clients with self-signed certificates in a dev sandbox.  
вЂў Creating simple SCRAM users and ACLs sufficient to isolate two service accounts for different topics.  
вЂў Understanding truststore vs keystore separation, and principal propagation to authoriser.  Full enterprise grade security design lives at higher proficiency.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
7. Observability & Troubleshooting
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў Exposing JMX metrics from brokers and clients; shipping them to a monitoring stack (Prometheus/JMX Exporter) and constructing basic dashboards for throughput, lag, latency, under-replicated partitions, offline partitions, request queue.  
вЂў Interpreting common performance or availability symptoms: exploding consumer lag, leader not available, offset commit failures, group rebalancing storms, disk full conditions.  
вЂў Using CLI or kcat/kafkacat to confirm end-to-end message flow, inspect headers, keys, offsets, and partition assignment.  
вЂў Capturing and comparing producer retries and consumer lag metrics to verify tuning adjustments.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
8. Ecosystem & Integration Awareness
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў High-level understanding of Kafka ConnectвЂ™s role; ability to deploy a pre-built source or sink connector (file, JDBC, S3, Elastic, etc.) and configure converters.  
вЂў Recognises where Kafka Streams, ksqlDB, Flink, Spark Structured Streaming fit in typical patterns, though deep stream-processing API fluency is not expected.  
вЂў Awareness of Cloud provider managed Kafka offerings (Confluent Cloud, MSK, Event Hubs for Kafka) and their implications on responsibility split, pricing levers, and feature parity.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
9. Delivery Practices & Collaboration
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
вЂў Embedding Kafka client configuration, topic creation scripts and operational runbooks into CI/CD pipelines and IaC manifests (Docker Compose, Terraform modules, Helm charts).  
вЂў Reviewing code written by peers for producer/consumer correctness, logging, error-handling and resource clean-up.  
вЂў Communicating topic design (naming, partition count, retention) and consumer-group strategy in design documents and code reviews.  
вЂў Participating in incident response by providing Kafka-specific diagnostics and remediation steps to SRE / DevOps personnel.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
10. Professional Boundaries
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
A BASIC-level practitioner is NOT yet accountable for:  
вЂў Holistic cluster sizing for tens of brokers, multi-AZ strategies, cross-datacenter replication, KRaft migration planning.  
вЂў Comprehensive security architecture, compliance and secrets management.  
вЂў Deep performance forensics involving page cache tuning, OS scheduler, JVM GC optimisation or network zero-copy adjustments.  
вЂў Advanced exactly-once transactional pipelines, custom Connect transforms, or production-grade stream-processing topologies.  
These fall into Intermediate, Advanced or Expert scopes.

Deliverable Capability Summary  
Given this scope, the practitioner can:

вЂ“ Stand up and operate a functional development or small production Kafka cluster.  
вЂ“ Design and develop reliable producer and consumer applications with correct partitioning, offset management and basic fault-tolerance.  
вЂ“ Perform routine topic and cluster configuration, monitoring, and first-line troubleshooting.  
вЂ“ Implement fundamental security controls and continuous delivery hooks.  
вЂ“ Collaborate effectively with more senior Kafka engineers by bringing sound foundational knowledge and cleanly executed operational tasks.

This constitutes the exhaustive competency boundary for BASIC proficiency in Apache Kafka.
